one_liner: The sponsorship IS a supermarket aisle.
search_doc:
The sponsorship IS a supermarket aisle.
Mechanisms: branded-utility-experience, sponsorship-inversion, retail-as-experience, participation-becomes-the-news
Idea: Set up a real working supermarket aisle deep in the forest at the 50km midpoint of the Kepler Challenge ultramarathon, branded 'Aid Aisle', where runners could grab whatever they needed.
Insight: Sponsors of endurance events usually pay to put a logo on the course; the most useful thing a supermarket can do is actually be on the course.

full_record.file_path: 2024/BRAND EXPERIENCE & ACTIVATION/BRONZE/Aid Aisle - Freshchoice (Dentsu Creative, Auckland).jpeg

extracted.tagline: 50km into New Zealand's most remote ultramarathon, when runners were hitting the wall, they hit a supermarket aisle.
extracted.context: As running sponsor, FreshChoice wanted to do more than slap their logo on the start line. They wanted to help runners finish. So they opened the Aid Aisle stocked with everything from electrolytes, bits of sugar or salt, to medicine, bandages, and even fresh socks.
extracted.idea: Set up a real working supermarket aisle deep in the forest at the 50km midpoint of the Kepler Challenge ultramarathon, branded 'Aid Aisle', where runners could grab whatever they needed.
execution: Branded 'Aid Aisle' supermarket aisle built at the 50km point in the forest
Stocked with electrolytes, sugar/salt snacks, medicine, bandages and fresh socks
Live aid station for runners of the Kepler Challenge ultramarathon
Brand presence at start/finish line and route signage

inferred.insight: Sponsors of endurance events usually pay to put a logo on the course; the most useful thing a supermarket can do is actually be on the course.
inferred.one_liner: The sponsorship IS a supermarket aisle.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Built a fully stocked FreshChoice supermarket aisle 50km into a remote New Zealand ultramarathon — a sponsorship that runners physically used to finish the race.

visual.style_description: Wide forest-floor hero photograph of a real FreshChoice 'Aid Aisle' supermarket aisle erected on a wooded mountain trail, with runners passing through; runner-quote callouts on the right, and a strip of additional documentation photos along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Building a fully-stocked supermarket aisle 50km deep into a remote New Zealand ultramarathon — replacing the usual logo-on-the-start-line sponsorship with the brand's actual product on the actual course — is a clean sponsorship-inversion that turns the sponsor into the utility runners physically need. One-line memorable in the runner-quote framing ('I thought I was hallucinating').

strategy.rationale: FreshChoice is a New Zealand supermarket chain (part of Foodstuffs) whose category positioning rests on local convenience; sponsoring the Kepler Challenge ultramarathon and showing up as an actual functioning aisle is on-mission for a supermarket brand whose product is, literally, the aid. The strategic insight that 'sponsors usually slap a logo, but a supermarket can actually help runners finish' is observationally true but not data-backed on the board.

execution.rationale: The build itself — an aisle of shelves stocked with electrolytes, snacks, medicine, bandages and fresh socks erected in remote forest at the 50km midpoint — is craft-precise and BXA-native. Channel reach is narrow by design: the Kepler Challenge is a single-event activation with a small participant base, and the board shows no documented PR or earned-media extension that would convert the on-the-ground stunt into a broader cultural moment.

impact.rationale: The board provides zero quantified impact metrics — no runner participation count, no aisle-use rate, no sales lift, no earned-media reach, no brand-tracker shift. The case rests entirely on three runner testimonial quotes ('Worth running over 2 hours for', 'I thought I was hallucinating', 'Like a mirage come true'), which is the weakest possible impact-evidence base. Business outcome for a supermarket sponsoring an ultramarathon is conspicuously absent.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the sponsor-as-actual-aisle inversion is a charming, BXA-native idea cleanly executed for a single ultramarathon — but with no quantified participation, reach or business outcome, the case sits on runner testimonials alone.

one_liner: Aldi launches Adidas, sells out in four hours.

Aldi launches Adidas, sells out in four hours.
Mechanisms: meme-as-product, lookalike-brand-baiting, social-listening-activation, limited-drop-experience
Idea: Aldi UK launched its own sportswear line, 'Aldidas', after fans began jokingly mashing up Aldi and Adidas branding online. The collection embraced the comparison head-on, daring rivals to take legal action — which became its own viral content.
Insight: When fans started joking online that Aldi should make Adidas-style gear, the brand had a chance to turn an internet meme into a real product drop.

full_record.file_path: 2024/BRAND EXPERIENCE & ACTIVATION/SILVER/Aldidas - Aldi UK (McCann Manchester).jpeg

extracted.tagline: Impossible for next to nothing.
extracted.idea: Aldi UK launched its own sportswear line, 'Aldidas', after fans began jokingly mashing up Aldi and Adidas branding online. The collection embraced the comparison head-on, daring rivals to take legal action — which became its own viral content.
execution: Aldidas-branded sportswear collection (hoodies and trainers)
Social posts featuring talent in the gear
Twitter/X reaction posts: 'Our lawyers said no to our new sportswear name, but they can't blame us if you say it…'
User-generated tweets — 'Aldidas!' — amplified the joke

inferred.context: Aldi UK has built a brand voice around cheeky social-first comedy and frequently plays on copycat humour with bigger brands.
inferred.insight: When fans started joking online that Aldi should make Adidas-style gear, the brand had a chance to turn an internet meme into a real product drop.
inferred.one_liner: Aldi launches Adidas, sells out in four hours.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Converted a viral social joke into a real sportswear drop that sold out in four hours, generated £1M in earned media and reignited Aldi's challenger comic positioning.

visual.style_description: Editorial fashion-style portrait of a model on graffiti-tagged London steps wearing the Aldidas hoodie and trainers; rough Aldi-yellow striped typography across the middle reading 'aldidas — impossible for next to nothing'; right side stacks press quotes, KPIs, and a smartphone tweet mock-up.

idea.rationale: Turning a fan meme ('Aldi should make Adidas gear') into a real Aldidas sportswear drop, then daring the rival to sue, is an on-brand challenger gag executed as a shoppable product rather than as a social post. The idea is recognisable rather than category-redefining — meme-to-product has been done — but the execution is unusually committed.

strategy.rationale: Aldi UK is the German-headquartered discount-grocery chain whose UK brand voice is built on cheeky social-first comedy and parody of premium competitors (the Cuthbert-the-Caterpillar lineage), so making a tongue-in-cheek Adidas knock-off is an exact-fit Strategy beat for who the brand is. The insight — that fans were already mashing up Aldi and Adidas branding online — is a real, observable cultural signal the brand acted on. The board does not disclose any audience-research base for the meme's scale, which keeps the insight at anecdote level.

execution.rationale: A real hoodies-and-trainers drop, plus reactive X posts ('our lawyers said no… but they can't blame us if you say it') and user-generated 'Aldidas!' tweets, give the social joke a retail spine. Channels are tight to where the meme lived (X, social, in-store) and the four-hour sell-out is consistent with limited-drop streetwear dynamics, but the orchestration stays inside Aldi's existing playbook rather than opening a new experience layer.

impact.rationale: '4 hrs to sell out' is the right business outcome for a limited drop and is the load-bearing proof point. '11M reach' and '880k engagements' are entrant-reported social claims rather than audited figures, and '£1M earned media' is an estimated AVE-style number whose base is not disclosed. The board does not say how many units were sold or what the cohort was, so 'sold out' lands as directional rather than scaled.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because it shipped the meme — Aldi turned a fan joke about Adidas into a real Aldidas drop that sold out in four hours, but the proof points lean on entrant-reported reach and the idea is challenger comedy more than category invention.

An NFL receiver's complaint becomes a 7-Eleven jersey.
Mechanisms: sponsorship-inversion, live-moment-as-stunt, slogan-recontextualisation, reactive-merch-drop
Idea: We created a celebration of Ja'Marr Chase and 7-Eleven's shared commitment to being 'Always Open' with a striking the-jamarr-chase / 7-eleven jersey activation and social campaign executed in less than 48 hours.

Following a few frustrating games where he was consistently open but didn't receive the ball, Ja'Marr allegedly took to social media and TV to express his frustration. Social rollout in under 48 hours of the news
Limited-edition T-shirt drop

USA Today — 'Ja'Marr warms up in 7-Eleven shirt'

inferred.insight: Ja'Marr's complaint that he was 'always open' on the field perfectly mirrored 7-Eleven's brand promise — the cultural moment was already half-branded.
inferred.one_liner: An NFL receiver's complaint becomes a 7-Eleven jersey.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hijacked a viral NFL controversy in under 48 hours with a 'Always Open' jersey and merch drop that became 7-Eleven's number-one seller and second-best-selling merchandise item ever, with 18M+ broadcast viewers.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial: hero shot of Ja'Marr Chase in a 7-Eleven branded chain-necklace tee with neon 'Always Open' script across the chest; three-column setup-idea-results layout with press tile quotes left, execution thumbnails bottom-left and metric blocks right with logo strip below.

idea.rationale: Latching onto Ja'Marr Chase's public 'I'm always open' complaint and converting it into a 7-Eleven jersey patch reads the cultural moment as already-half-branded and finishes the joke for the brand. The idea is reactive rather than originated — its strength is timing and pattern-match rather than novel mechanic.

strategy.rationale: 7-Eleven is the US convenience-store giant whose 'Always Open' positioning is literally its brand identity (24/7 store hours). The cultural alignment between Chase's complaint and the brand promise is exact, not borrowed. The insight — that the receiver's frustration mirrored the brand slogan — is observable and gave the team a real-time hook, though the alignment is coincidence-driven rather than strategy-driven.

execution.rationale: The 48-hour turnaround from news cycle to game-day jersey and merch drop is the executional craft — gameday wear by Chase delivered 18M+ broadcast viewers a branded sighting before social and retail picked up. The orchestration is reactive media plus a limited-edition T-shirt drop, which is sharp but light — there is no deeper experiential or live-event component beyond the broadcast moment and an e-commerce restock.

impact.rationale: The T-shirt becoming 7-Eleven's #1 seller and the campaign producing its second-best-selling merchandise item in history are concrete retail outcomes for a CPG brand, though the actual unit volumes and revenue aren't disclosed on the board. 500M social/editorial impressions and 99% positive sentiment are entrant-reported reach and sentiment claims; the broad sports-press logo bar (ESPN, USA Today, TMZ, Washington Post, Sky Sport) signals credible earned conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the brand caught a perfectly-aligned cultural moment and turned it into a record-selling merch drop in 48 hours, though the work is a reactive social-and-retail play rather than an originated brand experience.

search_doc:
The pitch-side board IS the delivery confirmation.
Mechanisms: sponsorship-inversion, real-time-broadcast-trigger, live-moment-as-stunt, branded-utility-experience
Idea: Ballboards turn pitch-side LED advertising boards into Mercado Libre delivery-themed messaging that appears at the exact moment a ball goes out of play, so fans literally see 'Your ball has arrived' the second the ball reaches the boundary.
Insight: A football out of play and a parcel arriving share the same physical mechanic — something passing the boundary — turning a broadcast moment into a brand demonstration.

full_record.file_path: 2024/BRAND EXPERIENCE & ACTIVATION/BRONZE/Ballboards - Mercado Libre (GUT Sao Paulo).jpg

extracted.tagline: The pitch-side ad board that made fans experience fast delivery.
extracted.idea: Ballboards turn pitch-side LED advertising boards into Mercado Libre delivery-themed messaging that appears at the exact moment a ball goes out of play, so fans literally see 'Your ball has arrived' the second the ball reaches the boundary.
execution: Live broadcast LED ads triggered by real-time ball-out events
Pitch-side boards in Brazilian football stadiums
Synchronised with delivery messaging during match broadcasts

inferred.context: Mercado Libre needed to demonstrate its delivery speed as part of its sponsorship of South American football, beyond passive logo placement.
inferred.insight: A football out of play and a parcel arriving share the same physical mechanic — something passing the boundary — turning a broadcast moment into a brand demonstration.
inferred.one_liner: The pitch-side board IS the delivery confirmation.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Used pitch-side LED boards as just-in-time delivery notifications synced with the ball going out of play, generating 1.6 billion impressions and 10x the engagement of standard match highlights.

visual.style_description: Wide shot of a Flamengo player at a packed Maracanã, the pitch-side LED reading 'mercado livre — Your ball has arrived' as the ball lands at the boundary; bright yellow 'BALLBOARDS' lockup above; metric bar and press-logo strip across the bottom.

idea.rationale: Triggering 'Your ball has arrived' on the pitch-side LED at the exact instant a ball goes out of play turns broadcast sponsorship into a real-time delivery demo — the boundary and the parcel share the same physical metaphor. The idea is sharp because the moment is unscripted: every out-of-play touch becomes a brand demonstration.

strategy.rationale: Mercado Libre is Latin America's largest e-commerce and fintech platform whose business case in the region is built on logistics speed, and the campaign sits inside its existing South American football sponsorship — so demonstrating delivery via the pitch-side board is exact category-fit rather than borrowed sport equity. The insight — that a ball leaving the pitch and a parcel arriving are the same physical mechanic — is a precise creative observation; what is missing on the board is any data point about the brand's delivery-speed claim that the activation is dramatising.

execution.rationale: Real-time-triggered LED messaging synced with live ball-out events in Brazilian football stadiums turns the existing sponsorship signage into a moment-by-moment broadcast device. The technical orchestration (live event detection + LED switch) is the campaign's craft load, and broad earned press across Latin American and European sports outlets (Marca, L'Equipe, ESPN, Olé, Clarín) extends the moment beyond match audiences.

impact.rationale: '1.6 BI impressions' and '40MM Brazil hour reach' are entrant-reported reach figures, and 'watched 10x more than match highlights' is a multiplier without the absolute base disclosed — '10x of what view count' remains open. '$6MM earned media' is an AVE-style estimate. No business outcome is shown — no delivery-volume lift, no MercadoEnvíos sign-up uplift, no share data in football-sponsorship markets — which is what a logistics activation ultimately needs to prove. The case rests on broadcast reach rather than on commercial behaviour.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the real-time LED trigger is a clever broadcast-sponsorship idea, but the case rests on impression and multiplier figures rather than on any delivery-business outcome for Mercado Libre.

one_liner: Bartending becomes a Heineken career credential.
search_doc:
Bartending becomes a Heineken career credential.
Mechanisms: hr-policy-as-campaign, linkedin-as-media, category-elevation, branded-utility-experience
Idea: Gen Z don't want to work in bars anymore — they want to work for companies and build a career. Heineken is one of the most attractive employers in Europe and the UK. For the past 3 years under Top Employer, Heineken changed its hiring policy and named Bar Experience as a vital skill in the hiring process across the entire Heineken Company. Simply put, working in a bar makes you ready to work in a bar.
Insight: Working in a bar is treated as a stop-gap on a CV; if the world's most desirable employer treats it as a credential, the whole job changes status.

full_record.file_path: 2024/BRAND EXPERIENCE & ACTIVATION/SILVER/Bar Experience - Heineken (LePub, Milan).jpeg

extracted.tagline: There is no experience like Bar Experience — a new company hiring policy to help bars find staff.
extracted.context: After the pandemic, a new major issue has now afflicted bars in Europe: a massive lack of staff. That's why Heineken, that's always been on the side of the HORECA industry, has decided to once again support bars.
extracted.idea: Gen Z don't want to work in bars anymore — they want to work for companies and build a career. Heineken is one of the most attractive employers in Europe and the UK. For the past 3 years under Top Employer, Heineken changed its hiring policy and named Bar Experience as a vital skill in the hiring process across the entire Heineken Company. Simply put, working in a bar makes you ready to work in a bar.
execution: Heineken top management as influencers on social media
Posted on LinkedIn with all the bars looking for staff where people could apply
First 3 months of bar work added to the Bar Experience skill on LinkedIn, allowing Heineken to filter out candidates for openings within the Heineken Company
metrics: +5K bar jobs applications in the first two weeks
100,000+ part-timers working in the hospitality industry since 2021

inferred.insight: Working in a bar is treated as a stop-gap on a CV; if the world's most desirable employer treats it as a credential, the whole job changes status.
inferred.one_liner: Bartending becomes a Heineken career credential.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned an internal hiring policy into a public solidarity gesture for European bars, lifting bartending into a corporate skill and driving 5,000+ bar job applications in two weeks.

visual.style_description: Cinematic warm-lit photograph of a young bartender behind a Heineken-lit bar serving as the hero, with white tagline lock-up bottom-left, three press-quote cards in the top corners, a right-hand column of context/idea/execution copy and a strip of green LinkedIn screenshots and partner logos along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Elevating bartending from a CV gap-filler to a recognised Heineken-Company hiring credential is a quietly elegant reframe that uses HR policy as the campaign asset rather than an ad. The mechanic is one-line memorable ('working in a bar makes you ready to work for Heineken') but lacks a single, photographable BXA moment, leaning more on the policy artefact than on a lived experience.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is the world's second-largest brewer and a Top Employer 3 years running in Europe and the UK, whose long-standing HORECA partnership story makes a 'side-of-the-bar' purpose entirely native rather than borrowed. Post-pandemic European staff shortages in bars are a documented structural problem the board frames as the strategic context. The 'Gen Z don't want to work in bars, they want a career' insight is plausible but cited without survey data or labour-market source.

execution.rationale: The orchestration runs through LinkedIn — Heineken top executives posting open bar roles, three months of bartending added as a skill recognised by Heineken's own ATS, and earned press across Dutch and Irish media — which is the right channel for a corporate-employer-brand audience. The execution is policy-led rather than experience-led, which is the BXA softness: the participation that matters happens on a job board, not in a live designed moment.

impact.rationale: +5,000 bar job applications in two weeks is the right participation outcome and the most concrete proof point on the board. The '100,000+ part-timers working in hospitality since 2021' figure is a long-running programme stat that predates this campaign window and is not isolated to it, so it overstates campaign-attributable impact. '90% of target audience reached' is a percentage without an absolute base (reached how many people?) and treated as a soft claim.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because elevating bartending into a Heineken hiring credential is a fresh use of HR policy as media, but the BXA-native participation case rests on job-board applications rather than a designed live experience.

one_liner: Beer loyalty points become a shopkeeper's pension.
search_doc:
Beer loyalty points become a shopkeeper's pension.
Mechanisms: loyalty-points-as-pension, branded-utility-experience, purpose-driven-product, government-partnership
Idea: In partnership with the Colombian Government and the Colombian Pension Fund (Colpensiones), we created the Beer Retirement Account, an initiative empowering shopkeepers to convert their unused BEES points into real money for real retirement.
Insight: According to the 2023 Global Retirement Index, Colombia's pension system is one of the world's worst. Approximately 82% of informal workers like shopkeepers will never retire, as reported by Colombia's National Administrative Department of Statistics (DANE). Poker, known as the beer brand of friendship, acknowledged this dire situation and decided to support the future of its key partners: the shopkeepers.

full_record.file_path: 2024/BRAND EXPERIENCE & ACTIVATION/BRONZE/Beer Retirement Account - Poker Beer (Mediamonks Hilversum).jpg

extracted.tagline: Savings for the future of those who had no future.
extracted.context: In Colombia, shopkeepers are essential to the economy, totaling 550,000 corner shops and representing 40% of the market share in the country, playing a significant role in the success of numerous mass-consumer brands.
extracted.insight: According to the 2023 Global Retirement Index, Colombia's pension system is one of the world's worst. Approximately 82% of informal workers like shopkeepers will never retire, as reported by Colombia's National Administrative Department of Statistics (DANE). Poker, known as the beer brand of friendship, acknowledged this dire situation and decided to support the future of its key partners: the shopkeepers.
extracted.idea: In partnership with the Colombian Government and the Colombian Pension Fund (Colpensiones), we created the Beer Retirement Account, an initiative empowering shopkeepers to convert their unused BEES points into real money for real retirement.
execution: Seeking a solution, Poker decided to make a small change on BEES, AB InBev's B2B platform already familiar to shopkeepers, where they buy beer for their businesses and earn points redeemable for prizes
Thousands of these points were going unused and expiring
Connected BEES with a special retirement savings account so shopkeepers, enabling each BEES point to be transferred to those accounts as real money
Similar to any retirement account, their money generates profits in the long term
+20% additional contribution by the Colombian Government
metrics: +12.5% Poker orders and increasing among shopkeepers
+20% additional contribution by the Colombian Government

inferred.one_liner: Beer loyalty points become a shopkeeper's pension.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Converted unused BEES loyalty points into real retirement savings for Colombia's informal shopkeepers, enrolling 10,000+ shopkeepers, banking 480,000 USD in pensions and lifting Poker orders 12.5%.

visual.style_description: Warm portrait of a smiling Colombian shopkeeper in her tienda holding a phone forms the hero, with gold serif 'Beer Retirement Account' title and partner logos overlaid centre, mocked-up retirement-account app screens upper-right, four columns of context/problem/observatory/idea copy and a yellow metric strip with shopkeeper portraits along the bottom.

idea.rationale: Converting unused B2B loyalty points on AB InBev's BEES platform into real retirement savings accounts for Colombian shopkeepers — partnered with Colpensiones and the Colombian government — turns a dormant beer-trade-marketing programme into a pension product. One-line memorable ('beer points become a pension') and original within loyalty-mechanic territory.

strategy.rationale: Poker Beer is AB InBev's leading Colombian beer brand, positioned as the 'beer of friendship', whose 550,000 shopkeepers represent 40% of the country's distribution share — both data points are sourced on the board and ground the strategic stake. The cited insight that 82% of Colombia's informal workers will never retire is anchored in DANE statistics and the 2023 Global Retirement Index — verifiable national-policy data, not invented. Mission-fit is solid because shopkeepers ARE Poker's core trade audience.

execution.rationale: The activation is a financial-product build: a software hook between AB InBev's existing BEES B2B platform and Colpensiones retirement accounts, plus a +20% government contribution. The orchestration is utility-led rather than experience-led — there is no designed live moment, retail experience or participated activation, which is the BXA softness. The partnership architecture (AB InBev × Colpensiones × Government) is credible heavy lift.

impact.rationale: 10,000+ shopkeepers enrolled, 271.4MM BEES points redeemed and 480,000 USD in net pension savings are right-shaped business outcomes anchored in absolute numbers, not just percentages. +12.5% Poker orders is a percentage without an absolute order-volume base. +21% BEES adoption among shopkeepers similarly needs an absolute denominator. No earned-media press wall is shown, so the cultural-conversation case is unreported.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because converting beer loyalty points into real pensions for 10,000 shopkeepers is a smart purpose-driven utility built on verifiable national pension data — but it reads more as a financial product than a designed brand experience, which caps it for BXA.

one_liner: Virtual blood becomes real blood donations.
search_doc:
Virtual blood becomes real blood donations.
Mechanisms: fiction-becomes-reality, in-game-reward-for-real-action, purpose-driven-product, behaviour-change-activation
Idea: Our 'Diablo Blood Harvest' campaign turned virtual blood into a life saving resource, making the act of donating blood a thematic extension of the game's narrative. As players donated, they not only engaged with the game's story on a deeper level but also contributed to a critical cause, helping to replenish national blood reserves.
Insight: Players already pour fake blood into Diablo for fun — the brand can convert that engagement into real blood for hospitals during a national shortage.

full_record.file_path: 2024/BRAND EXPERIENCE & ACTIVATION/BRONZE/Blood Harvest - Diablo (GUT, Los Angeles).jpeg

extracted.tagline: How the bloodiest game helped fight the blood shortage.
extracted.context: In 2023, the U.S. faced a severe blood shortage, prompting urgent calls for donations. Amidst this, Diablo IV was launching its new season, 'The Season of Blood', the bloodiest season to date. An opportunity to engage the gaming community in a meaningful way.
extracted.idea: Our 'Diablo Blood Harvest' campaign turned virtual blood into a life saving resource, making the act of donating blood a thematic extension of the game's narrative. As players donated, they not only engaged with the game's story on a deeper level but also contributed to a critical cause, helping to replenish national blood reserves.
execution: Blood donation PSA film 'A Public Service Announcement from Hell'
DiabloBloodHarvest.com platform linking players to donation centres
Social rollout via gamer creators and fan posts
In-game rewards unlocked per real blood donation (Bloodpetal Weapons, Lord Faith Mob Armor Set, Vermilion Eye Pieball Mount, Blood-Infused Gaming PC)
Real blood donation centre partnerships across the U.S.
Halloween-season multi-platform campaign
press_coverage: PC Gamer — 'Diablo IV wants your blood. No, your actual real-life blood'

inferred.insight: Players already pour fake blood into Diablo for fun — the brand can convert that engagement into real blood for hospitals during a national shortage.
inferred.one_liner: Virtual blood becomes real blood donations.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Converted Diablo IV's 'Season of Blood' into a real US blood donation drive, delivering 10K donations, 31.2M reach and 47.2K engagement during a national shortage.

visual.style_description: Dark gothic poster with a hellish bloody Diablo IV title type and a hero portrait of a red-eyed nurse holding an 'I donated blood' badge; left column carries Context / Idea body copy, right column shows social UGC tiles, big red metrics and in-game reward cards.

idea.rationale: Converting Diablo IV's 'Season of Blood' gameplay theme into a real-world blood donation drive — with in-game rewards unlocked by actual donations at clinics — turns the fiction-to-reality loop into a coherent purpose mechanic. The idea aligns a notoriously gory IP with hospital-grade utility in a way that legitimises a darker brand world.

strategy.rationale: Diablo IV is Activision Blizzard's flagship action-RPG franchise launching its 'Season of Blood' content drop during the 2023 US blood shortage. Tying virtual blood to real donations is an opportunistic but defensible mission move for a brand whose entire aesthetic is blood, even if 'purpose' is not Diablo's native register. The cited US blood shortage is a well-documented public-health fact in 2023, anchoring the contextual claim.

execution.rationale: The orchestration covers a PSA film, dedicated donation-platform website, gamer-creator social activation, and a partnership with real US blood donation centres tied to in-game cosmetic rewards (Bloodpetal weapons, mob armor set, Vermilion Eye mount). The mechanic depends on Halloween-season multi-platform sequencing and converts game players into clinic visitors, although the in-clinic experience layer is essentially standard donation rather than designed for the activation.

impact.rationale: 10K blood donations is the right behaviour-change outcome and an absolute number rather than a percentage — credit for naming the cohort. 31.2M reach and 47.2K engagement are entrant-reported figures and the only cited press is PC Gamer; the donations sit below the scale that would imply meaningful impact on a national shortage. No business outcome for the game itself (player engagement, retention, season-pass uptake) is shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the virtual-blood-for-real-blood loop is a credible purpose extension that converted 10K actual donations, though the scale is modest relative to a national shortage and the game-side business outcome is unstated.

one_liner: Flowers for men while they're still alive.
search_doc:
Flowers for men while they're still alive.
Mechanisms: cultural-norm-subversion, branded-product-line, purpose-driven-product, ritual-recontextualisation
Idea: This Valentine's Day, DoorDash and gender norms to rest and offered unique bouquets for men, calling them Bouquets For Living Men. These flower arrangements were designed to be given to a man on Valentine's Day — long before his funeral, while he's still living. The departed helped us launch the campaign with an online film where they sang about wishing they'd received flowers while they were still alive. A partnership with the NBA helped us promote the idea, and living men loved it, becoming a new Valentine's Day audience for DoorDash.
Insight: Men's funerals are the one moment society reliably gives them flowers — too late for them to enjoy.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the cereal-as-tarot ritual is a culturally-fluent Gen Z relevance move tied to a 37% sales lift, but the AR-and-livestream execution is a familiar playbook rather than a category-redefining experience.

Choosing Ferroviária — a smaller historic club — heightens the sentiment that 'real' team identity is being smothered by sponsorship.

execution.rationale: The activation lives where football fans actually consume the sport: a real match-day jersey reveal at the stadium with a before/after moment built around the Consul washing machine as the visible brand symbol. The campaign exists as a televisable live moment first and a press story second, with the wash-cycle as a coherent loop from product to club to fan to earned-media press release across Reuters, ESPN and Globo.

impact.rationale: +12% sales lift is the load-bearing business outcome for a washing-machine brand and it is anchored as a percentage rather than absolute units, but the rest of the metric stack relies on percentages without an absolute base — '+2300% brand mentions' and '+400% brand love' are unanchored from their baselines so the multiplier reads larger than it is provable. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the sponsorship-as-erasure reframe is exact mission-fit for a washing-machine brand and shows up as a real, photographable match-day moment with a measurable +12% sales lift behind it.

idea.rationale: Re-framing climate-strike absenteeism as a documented medical condition — with real environmental-science PhDs signing certificates diagnosing students as too sick to attend school in a hottest-year-on-record planet — is a legitimacy-hijack that converts a punishable act into a doctor-approved one. One-line memorable ('sick of climate inaction? get a sick note') and culturally precise to the 2019 Australian punishment context the campaign cites.

strategy.rationale: School Strike 4 Climate is the Australian arm of Greta Thunberg's global student-strike movement, whose mission is exactly student-led climate advocacy; the certificate is precise mission-fit and not borrowed purpose. The context — that thousands of Australian students were punished for striking in 2019, and that 2023 was the hottest year on record — is verifiable historical and climate fact. The legitimacy comes from a named signatory (Dr. Nick Abel, ANU Honorary Associate Professor), which anchors the artefact's credibility.

execution.rationale: The execution is a single downloadable PDF certificate with framed medical-style symptoms ('increased anxiety from Federal Government's climate policy inaction'), distributed ahead of climate strikes. As a BXA experience it is artefact-led rather than live-event-led — students download a document, they don't attend a designed activation. The orchestration extends through a global PR loop into UK, US and 26-country press but lacks experiential layers.

impact.rationale: 7,000+ downloads is the right participation metric and is anchored in absolute units, not percentages. $58M in earned media on $0 spend is entrant-reported and unaudited, and 2.1 billion impressions is a percentage-free but unverifiable reach claim. Coverage across 26 countries via BBC, Guardian, ABC, Sky and others is credible breadth. The real outcome a climate-strike movement needs — strike attendance, policy change, or schools updating their absence policy — is not documented on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the climate-doctor's-note is a culturally precise legitimacy-hijack with strong earned reach, but the impact case rests on impressions and earned-media valuations rather than evidenced strike turnout or policy shift.

idea.rationale: Building a tool that lets the public report incorrect sports-search results back to search engines themselves — using women's records as the entry point — is a fresh platform-jailbreak that turns gender bias into a tractable, click-by-click task. The idea is original because it does not protest the algorithm, it crowdsources its correction.

strategy.rationale: Team Heroine is a New Zealand-founded female athlete collective (built around former Black Ferns and footballers) whose mission is to surface women's achievement in sport, and partnering with the UN gives the work institutional standing rather than borrowed authority. The insight — that search engines invisibly demote female record-holders because click feedback loops favour male athletes — is anchored on real, checkable comparisons (Ronaldo's 119 international goals vs Christine Sinclair's 190), which makes the case verifiable rather than rhetorical. The Strategy is strong on mission-fit and insight veracity.

execution.rationale: A web tool listing 50+ incorrect searches with a one-click reporting interface, a hero film, a global panel discussion and endorsements from ESPN, CBS Sports, Andy Murray and Billie Jean King layer the campaign across digital product, content and high-credibility advocacy. The mechanism is genuinely participatory — millions of users submitting corrections — though the board does not detail how the corrections were aggregated, batched or actually delivered to search engines, which leaves the back-end half-visible.

impact.rationale: 'Millions of users reporting to search engines' names the right behaviour change as the outcome, but is a soft claim without a hard base — 'millions' could be 2M or 20M. '1B+ global reach' and '6M views on the hero film' are entrant-reported reach figures rather than audited ones. The Andy Murray and Billie Jean King quotes are a credible signal of cultural traction; what is missing is documented evidence that any incorrect search result was actually corrected — the proof of the verb 'correct' in the title.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Silver because it turns search-engine bias into a crowdsourced correction task with UN backing and athlete advocacy, but the impact case rests on 'millions' reporting rather than documented search-result changes.

execution.rationale: Trademark registration at the EU office, the multifunctional database + web crawler, an open-source listing constantly updated by users and partner companies, and automatic detection/removal across ten partner retailers stack the campaign as a working enforcement system rather than a comms layer. strategy.rationale: Poker is AB InBev's Colombian lager, a heritage mass-market beer brand whose distribution depends on tiendas (small neighbourhood shops) — so restoring those tiendas as community gathering rooms is direct mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The cited insight that only 4% of Colombian shopkeepers can afford furniture is a specific, plausible market truth that legitimises the brand-as-supplier role, though the board doesn't cite a source for the 4% figure.

execution.rationale: The system is implemented as a real, modular furniture set (tables, stools, sofas, benches) built from interlocking Poker crates plus a special connector, distributed in partnership with a folk Colombian design studio to participating small shops. Documentation on the board covers seven furniture configurations but the distribution scale, number of shops fitted out and live activation/event layer are not specified — the experience reads more as a product rollout than a campaign moment.

impact.rationale: +30% sales increase and +75% repeat purchases are anchored business metrics for a beer brand at the tienda channel, though both are percentages without an absolute base (lift across how many shops, against what baseline). '+46% with the same buyback' is opaque as a metric definition. No earned media is cited on the board, which limits the cultural conversation case and leaves the impact resting on retail-channel data alone.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the crate-into-furniture mechanic is exact for a brand whose distribution depends on the corner shop, although the impact case is sales-percentage only with no scale disclosure and no cited press.

idea.rationale: Manifesting Apple's SMS-downgrade behaviour as a physical 90s pager product mocks the rival's tech regression in a single, photographable object that reads instantly as 'this is what iPhone really is when texting Android'. The rival-troll-as-product move is sharp and visually durable, with the pager itself functioning as the campaign's anchor artefact.

strategy.rationale: Android is Google's mobile OS, the global market-share leader squeezed in the US by Apple's iMessage walled garden where 'green bubble' has become a cultural stigma. Trolling Apple over its refusal to adopt RCS is precisely on-brief for the brand's market position and not borrowed conflict — Google had a documented commercial stake in RCS adoption. The insight that the green-bubble divide is an Apple-engineered downgrade, not an Android limitation, is a defensible product-truth, though the campaign rests on framing it as a fairness argument rather than data.

execution.rationale: The campaign launches as a faux product page, mock tutorial content (set-up, send a GIF), social PR push and pickup including a Senator Elizabeth Warren mention, timed to hijack Apple's own product window. The orchestration is sharp for short-burst social/PR but exists primarily as a digital product page and content drip — limited physical or experiential layer, which is the BXA-category soft spot for what is essentially a press-and-social stunt.

impact.rationale: Apple's public commitment to adopt RCS two months after launch is a rare, verifiable cultural/business outcome — a competitor policy change directly attributable to the campaign's pressure, which is the strongest kind of impact a behaviour-change activation can claim. '1.2B impressions' and '+1012% social engagement' are entrant-reported reach claims, but the 200+ unique press stories across CNN, NYT, Forbes, TIME, Bloomberg and FT signal credible earned conversation; +9.6% iPhone negativity and +17.6% Android positivity are unbased shifts (of what survey, how many respondents).

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the iPager artefact pressured Apple into a public RCS commitment — a rare, verifiable rival policy change — though the work itself is closer to a PR-and-social stunt than a true brand experience.

idea.rationale: A clip-on device that turns any standard pen into a voice-to-letter learning tool — speak a word, see the letters on a screen, copy them onto paper — is a genuinely original product idea that inverts the literacy ladder (write before you read). The everyday-object transformation is precise: it bolts onto a pen anyone already owns, which keeps the artefact local and low-friction.

strategy.rationale: The World Literacy Foundation is an Australian-founded NGO working to end illiteracy in low-income communities, and an instructional device for non-readers is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The insight — that traditional teaching demands reading first, which is precisely the barrier illiterate adults can't cross — is plain and observable, anchored on the UNESCO figure of 773 million illiterate people worldwide. The case for veracity is strong on the social problem; the device's classroom efficacy is not data-backed on the board itself.

execution.rationale: Deployment with ESL teachers, dyslexic children and school dropouts gives the device three credible distribution pathways, and the three-step interaction (connect / speak / copy) is built for non-literate use rather than for a polished demo film. The execution is product-led rather than channel-heavy: the artefact is the campaign, and broad earned press (ABC, NBC, Yahoo, TNW, designboom) carries the awareness layer.

impact.rationale: '+2000 new devices by 2025' is a forward-looking deployment target rather than a documented result, and '+300 MM impressions in just two weeks' is an entrant-reported reach claim with no audited base. '100% positive sentiment' is a soft claim. The board names no learning outcome (literacy-rate improvement, words mastered, dropout-completion rate) which is the real business outcome a literacy product would need to prove — that absence limits the Impact ceiling even though the artefact is strong.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the device itself is a genuinely original branded utility that breaks the read-first barrier, but the Impact case rests on impressions and a 2025 deployment target rather than any documented literacy-learning outcome.

execution.rationale: Research films from the Cayman Islands and Peru, the open-sourced recipe, NotTurtle cooking classes for chefs and restaurants, a National Geographic documentary and social rollout across Instagram / TikTok / YouTube stack content, distribution and a real chef-training layer. The participatory experience (cooking classes) is the BXA-native hook, but the board does not disclose how many chefs or restaurants actually adopted the recipe — which is the difference between a documentary and a brand activation.

impact.rationale: '+190 publications' and '+1 billion impressions' are entrant-reported reach figures rather than audited ones, and '+95% positive sentiment' is a soft claim. The National Geographic documentary placement is a real, credible content win. The case does not show any restaurant adoption count, any actual turtle-soup substitution in a real market, or any sales / regulatory outcome — which is the business outcome a purpose-driven product launch needs. Impact rests on press visibility rather than behaviour change.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the AI-replicated turtle-soup recipe is exact-fit NotCo storytelling and earned National Geographic coverage, but the impact case shows no documented restaurant adoption or sales outcome behind the press visibility.

Idea: Paper Organs is an innovative product, following a common funeral custom in Taiwan — 'Joss Paper Burning'. People believe that creating paper money, houses, clothes and other paper replicas, then burning them in temples during rituals, allows these precious items to be sent to the deceased. execution.rationale: Hand-crafting paper replicas of hearts, kidneys, corneas, lungs and livers, distributing them through 11+ partner hospitals and temple ceremonies, and integrating them into actual funeral rituals is an experiential supply-chain build that respects the religious context. The roll-out is small in scale — 332 sets in six months — which is appropriate for a sensitive bereavement context but limits the BXA-native participation footprint.

impact.rationale: The behaviour-change outcome — 3,400+ organ-donation consent forms signed and 11+ hospitals joining the programme — is the right business outcome for a national registry and is anchored in absolute numbers, not just percentages. '76% of family members found comfort in their grief' is a softer self-report metric without an absolute respondent base. '+411% positive discussion volume' is a percentage without a stated baseline, which the rationale treats as a soft claim. The 332 sets of Paper Organs distributed shows the ritual artefact reach is small relative to Taiwan's donation problem.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because resolving a 2,000-year-old Chinese cultural taboo with the very ritual that caused it is a culturally precise idea — capped by the modest 332-set distribution and percentages without absolute bases on the comfort and discussion metrics.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is a single mechanical jersey redesign debuted on the PWHL's biggest stage, with the logo correspondingly moved in all of Molson's ads to preserve coherence — a tight, sponsorship-rights-native execution. execution.rationale: The system is implemented as permanent installations across the MinRicos district with venue-specific tactile codes (10 venue types from Restaurant to Hospital) embedded in the sidewalks outside actual partner premises, not as a one-off photo opportunity. impact.rationale: 75,000m² of installed area is a concrete, verifiable footprint; '500,000 people benefited' is the right behaviour-change framing for an accessibility intervention but the cohort definition (benefit measured how — by reach, by proximity, by users surveyed?) is not disclosed on the board. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the brand built a new layer of accessibility language out of its own product and committed to it as open-source civic infrastructure, even if the headline beneficiary count is left fuzzy.

strategy.rationale: Dramamine is the US's #1 motion-sickness medication, a 75-year-old over-the-counter brand whose business is the elimination of nausea — so taking credit for the obsolescence of the barf bag is direct mission-fit, not borrowed cultural relevance. The insight that the disappearance of an object proves the success of the antidote is internally consistent, though the board doesn't cite hard data on barf-bag attrition (which would be needed to literally claim Dramamine as the cause).

execution.rationale: The campaign assembles a Tribeca-selected documentary, OOH tribute posters, in-pharmacy retail takeover and an Amazon storefront takeover into a coherent multi-channel orchestration that lets the cultural artefact (the film) anchor commerce (the pharmacy/Amazon pickup). The decision to use only replica bags — never airline-branded ones — is a craft discipline that lets the campaign run without partner-clearance friction.

impact.rationale: The 26% Amazon sales lift is the right business outcome for an OTC medication and is anchored as a percentage rather than absolute revenue, but the absolute base isn't disclosed. The headline '650M+ impressions' and '10,000% above benchmark in social' are entrant-reported reach metrics, treated here as claims rather than verified facts — though the broad press logo bar (NYT, Hollywood Reporter, CBS, ABC, Entertainment Weekly, Billboard) is a credible signal of earned cultural conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the eulogy mechanic turns a 75-year-old OTC medication into a Tribeca-festival cultural artefact while still delivering a measurable 26% Amazon sales lift.

idea.rationale: Inviting firefighters into Renault's car-design process so the people who actually extract crash victims have a seat at the engineering table is a structural reframe of who the customer of a car-design programme is. strategy.rationale: Renault is the French automaker historically positioned around safety leadership (Euro NCAP testing pioneer), so co-designing vehicles with first responders is direct mission extension, not borrowed purpose. Naming Euro NCAP and the Italian Ministry of Interior as partners anchors the credibility above advertising-stunt territory.

execution.rationale: The programme is implemented as four concrete engineering features across new Renault models — not a one-off prototype — paired with an Amazon Prime documentary, a Kantar measurability award and continuing standards-development work with Euro NCAP and the Italian Interior Ministry. The orchestration spans product engineering, content, training, regulatory and PR layers, which is rare in BXA where most entries collapse into a single stunt.

impact.rationale: Euro NCAP adopting Renault's collaboration model into new safety standards, and the Italian Ministry of Interior rewriting its roadside-rescue operational doctrine, are concrete institutional/regulatory outcomes — far stronger than impression-counting. '230M earned media impressions' is an entrant-reported reach claim and is treated here as a directional rather than load-bearing signal. The board does not show vehicle-sales lift, which is the implicit business outcome for an automaker.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the programme delivered shipped safety features and rewrote a national rescue protocol — institutional outcomes most BXA work cannot claim — although the brand-experience layer for end-consumers is thin.

idea.rationale: Turning the existing Oreo twist ritual into a binary decision oracle — crème-left vs crème-right — gives the brand's defining gesture a new functional reason to exist. The idea is on-brand at a product level (you don't need a new SKU, just a new rule), which is what makes it scalable across 500+ executions and 13 languages.

strategy.rationale: Oreo, owned by Mondelez, is the world's best-selling cookie whose entire brand language is built around the 'twist, lick, dunk' ritual, so making the twist do something useful is exact equity-fit. The insight — that people are paralysed by everyday micro-decisions and outsourcing the choice is a playful escape — is plausible and culturally observable. The 'decisive 10,000 times a day' framing is unsourced on the board, and there is no audience data behind the decision-fatigue premise, which keeps it at intuitive level.

execution.rationale: 500+ unique outdoor / digital / print executions localised across 13 languages, a live wedding decided by an Oreo twist, real-time influencer reactions and real-life prompts ('explain your job to parents', 'CC them in every email') turn a single mechanic into a multi-market participatory platform. The orchestration is broad and consistent, though channel mix is heavily film/OOH-led and the participatory layer is mostly social rather than physical experience.

impact.rationale: '800,000+ decisions with a twist' is the right business outcome — documented participation — and is anchored on an absolute count. '1.9 BLN impressions' is an entrant-reported reach figure. '1 wedding' is anecdote rather than scaled behaviour change, and the 500+ executions figure is a production output rather than an outcome. The campaign does not disclose sales or share-of-occasion data, which is what an Oreo activation would ultimately need to prove against the cookie's category objective.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because it gives Oreo's defining twist gesture a new functional role across 800,000+ documented participations in 13 languages, but the case rests on participation and impressions rather than on a category-business outcome for the cookie.

The insight — that fantasy in-game currencies obscure real spending and have already pushed gamers into financial trouble — is plausible and culturally observable, though the board cites no quantified figures (industry revenue numbers, debt cases) to anchor the harm.

execution.rationale: The three-product stack (microsite, AI-tagged browser extension, mobile app) and the streamer-led distribution via HandOfBlood (1.1M+ followers) place the tool inside Twitch and YouTube where the buying decisions actually happen. impact.rationale: The board's metrics field is empty — no install numbers, no usage data, no documented financial-decision change. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the browser-extension overlay is a sharply on-brand financial-literacy tool for a savings bank, but the board names no usage numbers and rests on a single streamer endorsement.

idea.rationale: Hand-crafting 233 unique dolls — one per child on the UK transplant waiting list — and placing them in NHS medical waiting rooms where parents physically sit next to them, with a QR code that plays each child's real voice story, is an object-as-proxy idea that literalises the queue at the exact moment its target audience is captive. One-line memorable and audience-precision-targeted to a participatory degree few cause activations achieve.

The 233-dolls-for-233-children correspondence anchors the artefact's credibility in verifiable transplant-list data, which the NHS itself maintains.

execution.rationale: The orchestration is the build (233 unique handcrafted dolls), placement (NHS waiting rooms across the UK), QR-to-story per doll, and a sustained UK press push across national broadcast (BBC, ITV, Sky) and regional press. As a BXA experience the participation is highly intimate but contained — a few hundred dolls in waiting rooms, with most reach happening through earned media rather than direct doll encounters. The PR loop is heavy relative to the experiential footprint.

impact.rationale: +5,000 donor registrations in 3 months is the right business outcome for the NHS registry and is anchored in absolute units, not percentages. '1 life saved' is documented through a named recipient (the Tatham testimonial) — a single but verifiable outcome. 1.5bn impressions and 751 pieces of coverage are entrant-reported reach claims, with the press wall (BBC, ITV, Sky, Telegraph, Daily Mail, Mirror) credibly broad. The case rests primarily on registrations rather than transplant volume, which is the more upstream metric.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze, though the weighted score sits above the band — placing 233 dolls in NHS waiting rooms is a quietly devastating audience-precision idea that delivered 5,000+ new donor registrations, but its tight experiential footprint and reliance on earned media likely held the jury back from a higher medal.
